31: blasphemia tou pneumatos — blasphemy against the Spirit — uniquely among Matthew's sin-categories, this sin is ouk aphethesetai (will not be forgiven) in this age or the coming age. The context defines the blasphemy: attributing the works of the Holy Spirit (exorcism performed by the Spirit of God, v.28) to Beelzeboul (the devil). The sin is the persistent, seeing refusal to recognize the Spirit's presence in Christ's ministry — a willful inversion of light and darkness that places oneself permanently outside the reach of the Spirit who grants repentance.
35: ho agathos anthropos ek tou agathou thesaurou ekballei agatha — the good man out of his good treasure brings forth good things — thesauros (treasury, storehouse) as the inner reservoir from which speech flows; ekballo (to throw out, bring out) emphasizes that speech is not accidental but driven out of what is stored within. The heart-treasury precedes and determines the word: character produces speech, not vice versa.
1: en ekeinō tō kairō — in that season/time (kairos: a specific moment or season, not general time — chronos; this marks the Sabbath controversy as occurring within the theological moment of ch11's revelation/rejection discourse) — epeinasan (they were hungry — peinaō aorist: experienced hunger, the physical state that motivates the plucking): plucking grain by hand as a traveler was permitted under Deut 23:25; the controversy is about the day, not the act.
2: hoi Pharisaioi idontes eipon autō — the Pharisees seeing said to him — idou hoi mathētai sou poiousin ho ouk exestin poiein en sabbatō (look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath): the Pharisees address Jesus, not the disciples, establishing that the teacher bears responsibility for the disciples' practice. Ouk exestin (it is not lawful): plucking was classified by some rabbinic schools as harvesting (qatzir), one of the 39 prohibited labors.
3: ouk anegnōte ti epoiēsen Dauid — have you not read what David did (anagnōte aorist of anaginōskō: to read the Scriptures — Jesus challenges their knowledge of texts they claim to honor) — hote epeinasen autos kai hoi met autou (when he was hungry, he and those with him): hunger is the connecting term between the disciples' situation and David's. The argument is a fortiori: if David's need justified setting aside the restriction on the showbread, the disciples' need in the company of one greater than David (v.6) justifies their action.
4: tous artous tēs prothēseōs — the bread of the presence/the showbread (prothesis, setting forth, display — the twelve loaves arranged on a special table in the sanctuary, Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) — ho ouk exōn ēn autō phagein (which it was not lawful for him to eat — the same ouk exōn construction as the Pharisees' charge in v.2): the argument uses their own legal category. Ei mē tois hiereusin monois (except for the priests alone): the OT reference is 1 Sam 21:1-6 (where Ahimelech the priest gives David the bread).
5: hoi hiereis en tō hierō to sabbaton bebēlousin kai anaitioi eisin — the priests in the temple desecrate the Sabbath and are innocent (bebēlousin present of bebēloō, to make common, profane, desecrate — the root of the Fourth Commandment prohibition; the priests' temple-work on Sabbath technically "makes it common" in legal terms) — anaitioi eisin (they are without guilt/innocent): priests performing sacrifices and temple duties on the Sabbath are not guilty because the temple service overrides the Sabbath restriction. The argument: if the temple overrides Sabbath, and something greater than the temple is here (v.6), it overrides the Sabbath too.
6: legō de hymin hoti tou hierou meizon estin hōde — but I tell you that something greater than the temple is here (meizon neuter comparative: not "a greater one" — meizōn — but "something greater," making the reference to Jesus's presence indirect; Matthew uses the same neuter construction in v.41-42 for "greater than Jonah/Solomon") — the temple was the locus of the divine presence in Israel; to claim that something greater is present is to claim divine presence in a superior form.
7: ei de egnōkeite ti estin eleos thelō kai ou thysian — if you had known what this means: I desire mercy and not sacrifice (egnōkeite pluperfect of ginōskō: if you had come to know — a condition of fact implying they had not) — the citation of Hos 6:6 is used also in 9:13. Eleos (mercy, loving-kindness — the LXX rendering of Hebrew chesed, covenant-loyalty). Ou katediksate tous anaitious — you would not have condemned the innocent: the Pharisees' charge is not just legally mistaken but a violation of the very Scripture they uphold.
8: kyrios gar estin tou sabbatou ho huios tou anthrōpou — for the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath (kyrios tou sabbatou: the genitive of possession/authority — Lord over the Sabbath as an institution) — the Sabbath was given by God at creation (Gen 2:2-3) and at Sinai (Exod 20:8-11). To be its Lord is to claim the authority of its Giver. The statement closes the Sabbath debate not with a legal ruling but with a Christological claim.
9: kai metabas ekeithen ēlthen eis tēn synagōgēn autōn — and departing from there he came into their synagogue — autōn (their synagogue): the possessive pronoun marks increasing distance between Jesus and the Pharisees' institutions; "their" recurs in v.14. The synagogue setting for the withered-hand healing places the second Sabbath controversy in the formal liturgical-judicial space of the Pharisees' own domain, intensifying the confrontation.
10: kai idou anthrōpos cheira echōn xēran — and behold a man having a withered hand (xēra, withered, dried up: the hand has lost function and vitality) — kai epērōtēsan auton legontes (and they asked him, saying — epērōtaō aorist: a formal inquiry): the Pharisees frame the question as a legal test: ei exestin tois sabbasin therapeusai (whether it is lawful to heal on the Sabbath). Hina katēgorēsōsin autou (in order to bring charges against him — katēgoreō: the courtroom verb for formal accusation).
11: tis estai ex hymōn anthrōpos hos hexei probaton hen — which person among you who has a single sheep (probaton hen, one sheep — the singular emphasizes personal attachment) — kai ean empesē touto tois sabbasin eis bothynon (and if it falls into a pit on the Sabbath): rabbinic debates about rescuing animals on the Sabbath are attested in the Dead Sea Scrolls (CD 11:13-14 forbids it; later Pharisaic tradition may have permitted it). Jesus's question assumes the crowd would rescue the sheep, leveraging their instinctive compassion against rigid legal application.
12: posō oun diapherei anthrōpos probatou — how much more valuable is a person than a sheep (posō diapherei: by how much does it differ — a qal wa-chomer/a fortiori argument: if sheep rescue is permissible, human healing is more so) — hōste exestin tois sabbasin kalōs poiein (so then it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath — kalōs poiein: to do good, do well): the positive formulation transcends the narrow "healing" question into a general principle of beneficence on the Sabbath.
13: ekteison sou tēn cheira — stretch out your hand (ekteison aorist imperative of ekteinō, to stretch out) — kai exeteinen (and he stretched it out — the same verb; the action Jesus commands is itself part of the restoration): no spoken exorcism formula, just a command directed at the human body. Kai apokatestathē hygiēs hōs hē allē — and it was completely restored, as sound as the other (apokatestathē aorist passive of apokathistēmi: to restore to an original condition; the verb carries eschatological weight — the same root as apokatastasis, the restoration of all things).
14: exelthontes de hoi Pharisaioi symboulion elabon kat autou — the Pharisees going out took counsel against him (symboulion elabon: took counsel, made a plan — the first explicit mention of a plot to kill Jesus in Matthew) — hopōs auton apoleswōsin (how they might destroy him — apollymi aorist subjunctive: the same word used for perishing/destruction, now directed at Jesus): the Sabbath healing that reveals Jesus's identity produces a death plot in the same scene.
15: ho de Iēsous gnous anechōrēsen ekeithen — Jesus knowing withdrew from there (gnous aorist participle of ginōskō: knowing — perceptive knowledge of the plot; anechōrēsen aorist of anachorew: to withdraw, retreat — used of deliberate strategic withdrawal in Matthew) — kai ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi (and large crowds followed him): the withdrawal from the Pharisees does not diminish his following. Kai etherapeuse autous pantas (and he healed them all — pantas, all, without selection).
16: kai epetimēsen autois hina mē phaneron auton poiēsōsin — and he rebuked them so that they would not make him known (epetimēsen aorist of epitimaō, a strong rebuke — the same verb used for rebuking storms and demons; hina mē phaneron auton poiēsōsin: purpose clause, that they should not make him publicly visible) — the messianic secrecy command following mass healing sets up the Isaiah citation: Jesus's ministry matches the Servant's pattern of non-self-promotion.
17: hina plērōthē to rēthen dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou legontos — in order that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled (plērōthē aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō: Matthew's fulfillment formula, used 11 times) — the citation formula introduces the longest OT citation in Matthew, covering Isa 42:1-4. Matthew's version differs from both the Hebrew MT and the LXX, suggesting either an independent translation or a Christian recension emphasizing specific elements of the Servant portrait.
18: idou ho pais mou hon ēretisa — behold my servant whom I have chosen (pais: servant/child — the LXX word for the Isaian servant; ēretisa, aorist of hairetizō: to choose, elect — not the standard LXX term) — ho agapētos mou eis hon eudokēsen hē psychē mou (my beloved in whom my soul delights — agapētos: the same word as the baptismal/transfiguration voice; eudokēsen aorist of eudokeō: to be well-pleased): the language directly echoes the baptism (3:17) and transfiguration (17:5). Thēsō to pneuma mou ep auton — I will put my Spirit upon him (Isa 42:1).
19: ouk erisei oude kraugasei — he will not quarrel nor cry aloud (erizō: to dispute, contend; kraugazō: to shout, cry out loudly) — oude akousei tis en tais plateiais tēn phōnēn autou (nor will anyone hear his voice in the streets): Matthew's version of Isa 42:2. The Servant's ministry is defined by absence of public self-assertion: no street-preaching for display, no competitive religious argument. This explains the secrecy command of v.16: Jesus follows the Servant's pattern of quiet, non-self-promoting action.
20: kalamon syntetrimmenon ou kateaxei — a bruised reed he will not break (syntetrimmenon perfect passive participle of syntribō, to crush thoroughly: the reed is already crushed, not merely bent) — kai linon typhomenon ou sbesei (and a smoldering wick he will not extinguish — typhomenon present participle: actively smoldering; sbesei future): the Servant's work is not to eliminate the barely-surviving but to carry them through. Heōs an ekbalē eis nikos tēn krisin — until he brings justice through to victory (nikos, victory — stronger than the LXX's alētheia).
21: kai tō onomati autou ethnē elpiousin — and in his name the nations will hope (ethnē, nations, Gentiles — not just Israel; the Servant's reach is explicitly universal) — Matthew's version follows neither MT nor LXX exactly: MT has "islands will wait for his law"; LXX and Matthew have "in his name nations will hope." The Gentile scope of the Servant's mission is important: at the point where the Pharisees plot to kill Jesus, the citation looks beyond Israel to the nations who will hope in his name.
22: tote prosēnechthē autō daimonizomenos typhlos kai kōphos — then there was brought to him a demoniac who was blind and mute (prosēnechthē aorist passive: was brought — by others; typhlos kai kōphos: blind and mute, a double condition requiring a double healing) — kai etherapeuse auton hōste ton kōphon lalein kai blepein (and he healed him so that the mute man spoke and saw — hōste + infinitive: result clause): this miracle combines two of the messianic signs from 11:5, intensifying the Beelzeboul controversy that follows.
23: kai existanto pantes hoi ochloi — and all the crowds were astonished (existanto imperfect of existēmi, to stand outside oneself, to be amazed — the response is ongoing, not a single moment) — mēti houtos estin ho huios Dauid (could this perhaps be the Son of David? — mēti introduces a cautious question expecting an uncertain answer: "Could this possibly be the Son of David?"): an open question, not a firm conclusion. The crowds wonder; the Pharisees (v.24) answer with an accusation.
24: hoi de Pharisaioi akousantes eipon — but the Pharisees hearing said — houtos ouk ekballei ta daimonia ei mē en tō Beelzeboul archonti tōn daimoniōn (this man drives out demons only by Beelzeboul the ruler of demons): the accusation (first raised in 9:34) is escalated. Beelzeboul: the name-form in Matthew and Mark; probably from Baal-Zebul, "lord of the exalted house/dwelling." The logic: exorcism requires power over demons, which can come only from the demonic chain of command — unless the exorcist has a power that supersedes it.
25: eidōs de tas enthymēseis autōn eipen autois — knowing their deliberations he said to them (enthymēseis: inner deliberations, used in 9:4 for the scribes' inner reasoning about blasphemy) — pasa basileia meristheisa kath heautēs erēmoutai (every kingdom divided against itself is laid waste — erēmoutai present passive: is being deserted, becoming a wasteland): the three-part reductio (kingdom, city, household) applies a universal principle: internal division leads to collapse. If Jesus drives out demons by Satan, Satan is attacking his own forces.
26: kai ei ho Satanas ton Satanan ekballei — and if Satan drives out Satan (ekballei present: the accusation applied directly as a hypothesis) — eph heauton emeristhē (he is divided against himself — emeristhē aorist passive): the logical impossibility stated as a completed action hypothetically. Pōs oun stathēsetai hē basileia autou (how then will his kingdom stand? — stathēsetai future passive of histēmi): the question is rhetorical; implied answer: it cannot.
27: kai ei egō en Beelzeboul ekballō ta daimonia — and if I drive out demons by Beelzeboul — hoi huioi hymōn en tini ekballousin (by whom do your sons drive them out? — hoi huioi hymōn: your sons, your people — Jewish exorcists within the Pharisees' own circles): the argument turns the accusation back. If the criterion is that exorcistic power implies demonic alliance, then Jewish exorcists are equally implicated. Dia touto autoi kritai esontai hymōn — therefore they will be your judges: the Jewish exorcists will testify against the Pharisees' inconsistency.
28: ei de en pneumati theou egō ekballō ta daimonia — but if it is by the Spirit of God that I drive out demons (pneumati theou: the Spirit of God — Matthew's version; Luke 11:20 has "finger of God," which may be the original Q form, echoing Exod 8:19) — ara ephthasen eph hymas hē basileia tou theou (then the kingdom of God has come upon you — ephthasen aorist of phthanō: to arrive before expected, to arrive already): the kingdom has come upon them without them recognizing it. Spirit-empowered exorcism is the presence of the eschatological kingdom, not a demonstration but the thing itself.
29: ē pōs dynatai tis eiselthein eis tēn oikian tou ischyrou — or how can anyone enter the house of the strong man (ischyros, strong/powerful — the same word used in 3:11 for John's description of Jesus as "mightier than I") — kai ta skeuē autou harpasai ean mē prōton dēsē ton ischyron (and carry off his possessions unless he first binds the strong man — dēsē aorist subjunctive of deō: to bind): the parable defines Jesus's exorcistic ministry as the "binding" of Satan, making possible the rescue of those under his dominion. Kai tote tēn oikian autou diarpasei — and then he can plunder his house.
30: ho mē ōn met emou kat emou estin — the one not with me is against me (mē ōn met emou present participle: the one who is not being with me, continuously not aligned) — kai ho mē synagōn met emou skorpizei (and the one not gathering with me is scattering — skorpizei present: is in the act of scattering): the gathering/scattering image recalls the shepherd (9:36; Ezek 34). No neutral position exists relative to Jesus's mission — not-gathering is actively scattering. This frames the Beelzeboul controversy: the Pharisees' accusation is not neutral observation but active opposition.
32: kai hos ean eipē logon kata tou huiou tou anthrōpou — and whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man (logos kata: a word directed against — the legal register) — aphethēsetai autō (it will be forgiven him — future passive divine): speech against Jesus-in-his-humiliation is forgivable. Hos d an eipē kata tou pneumatos tou hagiou ouk aphethēsetai autō oute en toutō tō aiōni oute en tō mellonti — whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, neither in this age nor in the age to come (toutō tō aiōni...tō mellonti: the two-age schema of Jewish eschatology; the Rabbinic olam ha-zeh and olam ha-ba).
33: ē poiēsate to dendron kalon kai ton karpon autou kalon — either make the tree good and its fruit good (poiēsate aorist imperative of poieō: make, treat as — the command is logically ironic since one cannot simply make a tree good by willing it) — ē poiēsate to dendron sapron kai ton karpon autou sapron (or make the tree bad and its fruit bad — sapros: rotten, decayed): you must choose your category consistently. The Pharisees cannot attribute good fruit (healing) to a bad tree (demonic source). Ek gar tou karpou to dendron ginōsketai — for by the fruit the tree is known: the tree-fruit principle inverted from 7:16-20.
34: gennēmata echidnōn — brood of vipers (gennēmata echidnōn: offspring of vipers — the same address as John's to the Pharisees and Sadducees in 3:7): the term combines origin and character. Pōs dynasthe agatha lalein ponēroi ontes (how are you able to say good things being evil? — ponēroi ontes present participle: constitutively evil): the Pharisees cannot produce good assessment because they are bad trees. Ek gar tou perisseumatos tēs kardias to stoma lalei — for the mouth speaks from the overflow of the heart (perisseuma: overflow, abundance — what pours over from a full container).
36: pan rēma argon ho ean lalēsōsin hoi anthrōpoi — every idle/ineffective word that people speak (rēma argon: idle word — argos literally means not-working, inactive, idle; it can mean useless or irresponsible; in context, words spoken against the Spirit-empowered ministry of Jesus "do not work" because they misidentify what is happening) — apodōsousin peri autou logon en hēmera kriseōs (they will give an account about it on the day of judgment — apodōsousin logon: to render account, a judicial phrase).
37: ek gar tōn logōn sou dikaiōthēsē — for by your words you will be justified/acquitted (dikaiōthēsē future passive of dikaioō: to be declared righteous, acquitted — the forensic term used here in a strict judicial sense: your words form the basis of the verdict) — kai ek tōn logōn sou katadikasthēsē (and by your words you will be condemned — katadikasthēsē future passive of katadikō): the forensic parallelism is exact. The speech-judgment principle addresses the fruits of the heart (v.34-35) that reveal what one truly is.
38: tote apekrīthēsan autō tines tōn grammateōn kai Pharisaiōn — then some of the scribes and Pharisees answered him — didaskale thelomen apo sou sēmeion idein (teacher, we want to see a sign from you — sēmeion: a sign, a wonder-as-proof): the request for a sign follows the Beelzeboul controversy. Having explained away the miracles as demonic, they now request a different, unmistakable sign. Thelomen (we want): the verb of volition signals what drives the request — not openness to evidence but a demand for evidence on their own terms.
39: genea ponēra kai moichalis sēmeion epizētei — a wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign (ponēra kai moichalis: wicked and adulterous — moichalis is the prophetic term for covenant-unfaithfulness, Israel as an adulterous wife to YHWH; Hos 1-2; Jer 3:8; Ezek 16) — kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna tou prophētou (and a sign will not be given it except the sign of Jonah the prophet — dothēsetai future passive divine): the single exception is the Jonah-sign, which will not be recognized even when it comes.
40: hōsper gar ēn Iōnas en tē koilia tou kētous treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas — for as Jonah was in the belly of the sea-monster three days and three nights (kētos: a great sea-creature; treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas: citing Jonah 1:17 LXX) — houtōs estai ho huios tou anthrōpou en tē kardia tēs gēs treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas (so will the Son of Man be in the heart of the earth — en tē kardia tēs gēs: the heart/center of the earth, echoing Ps 46:2; = burial): the Jonah typology applies not to Jonah's preaching but to his entombment and emergence. The only sign given is death and resurrection.
41: andres Nineuitai anastēsontai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinousin autēn — the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it (anastēsontai future of anistēmi: will rise up — the resurrection verb used for the judgment-rising of witnesses) — hoti metenoēsan eis to kērygma Iōna (for they repented at the preaching of Jonah — metenoēsan aorist: Nineveh's repentance was complete and definitive). Kai idou pleion Iōna hōde — and behold, something greater than Jonah is here: the neuter pleion (greater thing; cf. v.6,42) makes the comparison structural, not merely personal.
42: basilissa notou egerthēsetai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinei autēn — the Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it (basilissa notou: the Queen of the South, the Queen of Sheba from 1 Kgs 10:1-13) — hoti ēlthen ek tōn peratōn tēs gēs akousai tēn sophian Solomōntos (for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon — peratōn tēs gēs: the ends of the earth): a Gentile woman traveled the maximum distance to hear Israel's greatest king. Kai idou pleion Solomōntos hōde — and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.
43: hotan de to akatharton pneuma exelthē apo tou anthrōpou — when the unclean spirit goes out of a person (to akatharton pneuma: the unclean spirit — akatharton is the ritual purity word applied to the spiritual realm) — dierchetai di anydron topōn zētoun anapausin kai ouch heuriskei (it passes through waterless places seeking rest and does not find it — anydros: waterless, arid; the wilderness/desert as the haunt of evil spirits is a Second Temple Jewish belief; anapausin: rest, the same word as 11:28-29): the returning-spirit parable addresses the generation's spiritual condition.
44: tote legei eis ton oikon mou epistrepsō — then it says: I will return to my house (epistrepsō future: will return; the spirit claims the person as its "house") — kai elthon heuriskei scholazonta sesarōmenon kai kekosmēmenon (and coming it finds it standing empty, swept, and decorated — scholazonta present participle of scholazō: to be idle, empty, unoccupied; sesarōmenon perfect passive: having been swept; kekosmēmenon perfect passive: having been put in order/decorated): the danger is not the initial exorcism but the failure to fill the swept house. Empty order is not security.
45: tote poreuetai kai paralambanei meth heautou hepta hetera pneumata ponērotera heautou — then it goes and takes with it seven other spirits more wicked than itself (hepta: seven, the number of completeness; ponērotera comparative: more evil) — kai eiselthonta katoikei ekei (and entering they dwell there — katoikei present: settled, permanent dwelling): the final state is quantitatively and qualitatively worse. Kai ginetai ta eschata tou anthrōpou ekeinou cheirona tōn prōtōn — and the last things of that person become worse than the first: ta eschata (last things, end condition) is eschatological vocabulary applied to the individual. Houtōs estai kai tē genea tautē tē ponēra — so also it will be with this wicked generation.
46: eti de autou lalountos tois ochlois idou hē mētēr kai hoi adelphoi autou heistēkeisan exō — while he was still speaking to the crowds, behold his mother and brothers stood outside (heistēkeisan pluperfect of histēmi: had been standing — they were already there before this moment) — zētountes autō lalēsai (seeking to speak to him): the arrival of Jesus's biological family during a discourse on spiritual family creates the contrast that vv.48-50 resolve. The placement intercalates the physical family's interruption to allow the definition of the true family.
47: eipen de tis autō — someone said to him — idou hē mētēr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou exō hestēkasin zētountes soi lalēsai (behold, your mother and your brothers are standing outside, seeking to speak to you): the messenger's message uses the natural kinship vocabulary (hē mētēr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou: your mother and your brothers) that Jesus is about to redefine. The verse is omitted in some manuscripts (Codex Sinaiticus) but has strong textual support and is parallel to Mark 3:32.
48: ho de apokritheis eipen tō legonti autō — but he answering said to the one speaking to him — tis estin hē mētēr mou kai tines eisin hoi adelphoi mou (who is my mother and who are my brothers?): the rhetorical question is not a dismissal of Mary but a redefinition of the category "family." Jesus does not deny the biological family; instead he asks a question that opens the categories of mētēr (mother) and adelphos (brother) beyond biological definition to a new criterion.
49: kai ekteinas tēn cheira autou epi tous mathētas autou eipen — and stretching out his hand toward his disciples he said (ekteinas tēn cheira autou epi: stretching out his hand toward — the same gesture-verb ekteinō used for healing the withered hand in v.13; here it is a gesture of identification) — idou hē mētēr mou kai hoi adelphoi mou (behold my mother and my brothers): the gesture makes the answer visible before the verbal declaration. The disciples present, not the family outside, constitute Jesus's nearest family.
50: hostis gar an poiēsē to thelēma tou patros mou tou en ouranois — for whoever does the will of my Father in heaven (poiēsē aorist subjunctive of poieō: the general condition; to thelēma tou patros mou: the will of my Father, not the law abstractly but the Father's specific will) — autos mou adelphos kai adelphē kai mētēr estin (that person is my brother and sister and mother): the family-triad (brother, sister, mother) is now defined by the doing-of-will, not by birth. Father-will is the membership criterion for Jesus's family; the present continuous sense is implicit in the conditional construction.
